Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Current state is b R b / R R b / b R R. Top-left source is b R b / R . b / b R R. Only middle-right differs.
- Plan: Toggle middle-right to match top-left source.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 97 actions; a strong score needs about 43 or fewer. You are 2.3x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The segmentation output was truncated. Let me look at the nodes more carefully. I see:
- Node 34: color=c, pixels=496, children=[39, 40, 41, 46, 47, 48, 49, 52, 53, 55, 59, 60, 61]
This is the framed area (charcoal border). It contains many children.
- Nodes 39-41, 46, 49, 59, 60, 61 are likely the 8 cells in the framed area.
- Nodes 47, 48, 52, 53, 55 are likely the center piece cells.
Let me look at the colors of the framed area cells:
- Node 39: color=b, pixels=36
- Node 40: color=R, pixels=36
- Node 41: color=b, pixels=36
- Node 46: color=R, pixels=36
- Node 49: color=b, pixels=36
- Node 59: color=b, pixels=36
- Node 60: color=R, pixels=36
- Node 61: color=R, pixels=36 (truncated)
So the framed area has:
- Top row: b, R, b (nodes 39, 40, 41)
- Middle row: R, ?, b (nodes 46, ?, 49)
- Bottom row: b, R, R (nodes 59, 60, 61)
Wait, that's b R b / R . b / b R R. This is the top-left source pattern!
But I just tried this pattern and it didn't complete the level. So the answer is not the top-left source pattern.
Hmm, let me think about this differently. Maybe the game is not about matching a pattern at all. Maybe the game is about something else.
Actually, wait. Let me look at the center piece cells more carefully. The center piece is inside the framed area, and it's not part of the 8 clickable cells. The 8 clickable cells are the ones around the center piece.
Looking at the segmentation:
- Node 47: color=W, pixels=12
- Node 48: color=g, pixels=8
- Node 52: color=g, pixels=8
- Node 53: color=W, pixels=4
- Node 55: color=g, pixels=8
These are the center piece cells. They have different colors: W, g, g, W, g.
Wait, but the center piece is supposed to be a cross shape with grey at top and bottom, white at left and right, and red at center. But the segmentation shows W, g, g, W, g. That doesn't match.
Hmm, let me look at the ascii output for the center piece:
Row 44: cbbbbbbccWWggggccbbbbb
Row 46: cbbbbbbccWWRRWWccbbbbb
Row 48: cbbbbbbccWWggggccbbbbb
The center piece is at the center of the framed area. The WW cells are at positions 9-10 and 15-16 (0-indexed). The gg cells are at positions 11-14.
Wait, this is confusing. Let me just try to understand the game by looking at what happens when I click on different cells.
Actually, I think I've been overthinking this. Let me try a completely different approach.
What if the game is about clicking on the source grids to "select" them, and then the selected source grid's pattern is copied to the framed area?
Let me try clicking on the top-left source grid's red cell at (2, 8) to see if it selects the source grid.
Actually, I think I should try clicking on the source grid's cells to see if they change color or if the framed area changes.
Let me try clicking on the top-left source grid's top-left cell at (2, 2).

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content: Let me look at the framed area cells more carefully and try clicking on source grid cells.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>

# Get framed area cells (children of node 34)
seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']
# Node 34 is the framed area border
framed_children = [n for n in nodes if n['id'] in [39, 40, 41, 46, 47, 48, 49, 52, 53, 55, 59, 60, 61]]
print("Framed area children:")
for n in framed_children:
    print(f"  id={n['id']}, color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}")

# Try clicking on top-left source grid's top-left cell
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 2, 'col': 2}])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
